places.passage: Indian-Irish cosy crime. Semi-retired Irish nun Sister Agatha and Indian hotel owner Avtar Mehta roam the Chandni Chowk district to outwit the police and catch a killer, while not losing their lives or their sense of humour. Published in India by Penguin India. Published in the UK and ireland by the No Exit Press imprint of Bedford Square publishers. Published in audiobook by WF Howes and narrated by Paul Tylak. This is the first in the Havel Hotel Mysteries series.

places.author: Suhit Sen

places.author: Kushwant Singh
places.passage: Considered Kushwant Singh's vast, erotic, irreverent magnum opus on the city of Delhi, this novel travels through time, space and history to 'discover' his beloved city. Penguin Books, Translated into Urdu by Irfan Ahmad Khan

places.author: Puja Mukherjee Khattri
places.passage: A thriller about the lengths people will go to, in order to control the lives of others. And how they use an online game to run interference in their lives.

places.author: Shraddha Sahi
places.passage: Historical fiction about how a group of children from Naikwadi, near Pune help Tanaji to win the battle of Kondana

places.author: Shikhar Goyal
places.passage: A comedy thriller about an Indian origin DJ encountering gruesome challenges while trying to win his girlfriend back.

places.author: Vijaydan Detha
places.passage: Folktales from rural life in Rajasthan. It has oldest and most popular fables from Marwar region of Rajasthan. Penguin Puffin Classics. Stories in this complilation were orginally written by Detha in Rajasthani language and later translated into English by Vishesh Kothari.

places.bookTitle: The Garden of Tales: The Best of Vijaydan Detha
places.author: Vijaydan Detha
places.passage: Collection of folk stories set in the Marwar region in Rajasthan. These tales depicts rural life, folk wisdom and moral lessons through imaginative and culturally rich stories. Harper Perennial. Stories in this complilation were orginally written by Detha in Rajasthani language and later translated into English by Vishesh Kothari.

places.author: Chetan Bhagat
places.passage: corruption in the Indian educational system, the struggle of youth to find success, and a complicated love triangle in Varanasi. Rupa Publications

places.bookTitle: Journey to the Hills and other Stories
places.author: Divyaroop Bhatnagar
places.passage: Nostalgic family memoir set in 1960s Mussoorie Notion Press

places.author: Divyaroop Bhatnagar
places.passage: Murder Mystery with two time lines set in the British Raj and the 1970s Om Books International

places.author: Divyaroop Bhatnagar
places.passage: Collection of short stories based on the old houses of Mussoorie set in the 1960s Om Books International

places.author: Jhumpa Lahiri
places.passage: Indian Diaspora, Konark Sun Temple Indian Publisher: HarperCollins Publishers India

places.passage: An Odia romance fiction that is set against the dilemma of marriage and love. Cuttack Students' Store

places.author: Shirley Lalrinfeli
places.passage: A coming-of-age story of two friends, navigating their lives and friendship as one faces a personal battle with mental health while the other struggles with addiction. The book is published under Fraser Media and Publication.

places.author: Sarat Chandra Chattopadhyay
places.passage: Set in sometime between late 19th-century to early 20th century,[6] the story occurs in different regions of British India — Bhagalpur, Patna, Rangoon, Sainthia (Birbhum) and Debanandapur (Hooghly)

places.author: Sakoon Singh
places.passage: Layered narrative wherein twenty something Mehr tries to unravel the heart rending story of the separation of Rani Jind Kaur and her son Duleep. Multiple strands come together from historical and contemporary times. Rupa

places.author: Easterine Kire
places.passage: Female empowerment, folklore, legend, patriarchy Speaking Tiger

places.author: Kamal Desai
places.passage: A spectacular classic about womanhood and self-definition Speaking Tiger

places.author: Rajendra Banahatti
places.passage: A wry, humane meditation on ageing and memory. Speaking Tiger

places.bookTitle: The Cold War of Sadanand Borse
places.author: Shyam Manohar
places.passage: social satire, middle class dream Speaking Tiger

places.author: Vyankatesh Madgulkar
places.passage:
A powerful, character-driven rural classic about a young teacher’s
journey into an isolated shepherd village to bring education and
progress to a forgotten community. Speaking Tiger

places.author: D.B. Mokashi
places.passage: A poignant meditation on tradition, migration, memory, and the unravelling of a world where faith once held family and community together. Speaking Tiger

places.bookTitle: I Met a Man Who Wasn't There
places.author: Sakyajit Bhattacharya
places.passage: cozy, locked door murder mystery Speaking Tiger

places.bookTitle: Tall Tales By a Small Dog
places.author: Omair Ahmad
places.passage: A funny, quirky, unputdownable chain of stories about the heroes, villains and oddballs of Gorakhpur. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Madhulika Liddle
places.passage: Speaking Tiger

places.author: Madhulika Liddle
places.passage:
Spanning the years from the early 1400s to 1556, An Unholy Drought weaves an intricate tale
of varied characters and their loves, their lives, their triumphs and tribulations, in Delhi, as
the city transitions from the last days of the Sultanate to the dawn of the Mughal Empire. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Namita Gokhale
places.passage: A haunting novel about female friendship, loneliness and ageing—set in the Himalayan region of Kumaon Speaking Tiger

places.author: Imayam
places.passage: An unnerving and brilliant tale about obsessive love. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Damodar Mauzo
places.passage: A tender coming-of-age story of a boy who learns to love. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Hamza Jalil Albasit
places.passage:
This family saga and political epic is a genre-bending literary fiction about Karachi’s young
assassins. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Tahira Naqvi
places.passage: An exquisite novel, set in Lahore during the Zia-ul-Haq regime, about a young history teacher who becomes an activist. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Tahira Naqvi
places.passage:
A hard-hitting expression of women liberation and equal rights—set in Pakistan during
and post the Zia-ul-Haq regime. Speaking Tiger

places.bookTitle: The Lady on the Horse and Other Secrets
places.author: Ramona Sen
places.passage: A gripping family saga that captures the essence of Calcutta and its bhadralok. Speaking Tiger

places.bookTitle: The Ex Daughters of Tolstoy House
places.author: Arunima Tenzing Tara
places.passage:
A superb Gothic novel set in the
bland calm of Lutyens’ Delhi. Speaking Tiger

places.author: R. Raj Rao
places.passage: A funny, subversive, provocative novel about an inter-faith gay affair Speaking Tiger

places.author: Anjali Deshpande
places.passage: A crime fiction which holds up a mirror to the reality of Indian society—the many struggles of caste and class in a deeply unequal country. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Inez Barney
places.passage: A brilliantly inventive novel on the idea of India as a nation, seen through the eyes of a Turkish intellectual and freedom fighter. Speaking Tiger

places.bookTitle: Needle in the Bottom of the Sea: Classic Bengali Tales From the Sunderbans
places.author: Translated by Tony K. Stewart
places.passage: Enchanting pir kathas, or stories about Sufi saints, from early modern Bengal, which show how Hindu and Muslim religious traditions converged to create the syncretic culture we know today. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Bhaichand Patel
places.passage: A compelling, richly human and heartwarming story about old worlds giving way to new, and love across the barriers of religion. Speaking Tiger

places.bookTitle: The Women Who Would Not Die: Stories
places.author: Uddipana Goswami
places.passage: Twelve masterful stories from Assam that give a vivid picture of life in a conflict zone, in prose that is poetic and powerful in equal measure. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Manzu Islam
places.passage: Set in the period before and through the 1971 war of independence, this immersive and powerful novel is the epic of Bangladesh. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Manzu Islam
places.passage: One of the most daring novels written about the atrocities and communal violence that led to the creation of Bangladesh. Speaking Tiger

places.bookTitle: One More Story About Climbing a Hill
places.author: Davabrata Das
places.passage: Powerful and evocative stories about everyday life in Assam, in a time of conflict and crime. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Tsering Namgyal Khortsa
places.passage: A beautiful novel about the life ofTibetans outsideTibet today. Speaking Tiger

places.bookTitle: Sinema: The Bollywood Bungle of Andy Duggal
places.author: Vikram Singh
places.passage:
A whirlwind journey of chaos, scams, madness, moments of absurd luck, and the intricacies of Bollywood that gives you an eye-popping
picture of the film industry. Speaking Tiger

places.bookTitle: The Death of a Sarus Crane: Sudha Gupta's Adventures in Detection
places.author: Ambai
places.passage: Four long stories of detection which is a vivid blend of mystery and cultural insight. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Madhulika Liddle
places.passage: A sensitive, light-hearted romance that celebrates love in all its unpredictable ways. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Sheela Rohekar
places.passage: A poignant and passionate fiction offering a deep-dive into the characterful lives of six generations of a minor Jewish community living in India—the Bene Israel. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Angshu Dasgupta
places.passage: Set in 1980s’ Calcutta. A tender, moving and immersive story of a young boy’s journey through conflict and confusion to self-realization and strength. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Usha Priyamvada
places.passage: Explores the physical, mental and social paradigms which locked so many women into narrow ideals, as they still do. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Godfrey Joseph Pereira
places.passage: Based on true events. An utterly gripping novel?the disturbing, astonishing story of an Englishman who won everything but lost his soul on a no man’s land in the Arabian Sea. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Sujit Saraf
places.passage:
A searing examination of what it means to be Indian today, it
raises pertinent questions about nationhood, citizenship, and the lot of those
who live on the margins. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Greta Rana
places.passage: Inspired by real-life events, Hostage is the moving story of one of the thousands who flee the social and political upheaval of their country in search of a better life. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Shakoor Rather
places.passage: Gives us an insider’s view to everyday life and emotions in the conflict-ridden valley. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Shraddha Ghale
places.passage: It is a coming of age story of a girl in 1990s Kathmandu during the Nepalese civil war. Speaking Tiger

places.author: Kiran Nagarkar
places.passage: A bawdy, picaresque novel about two boys born in Mumbai chawl Penguin

places.author: Tejaswini Apte Rahm
places.passage: A rags-to-riches story set in colonial Bombay Aleph

places.author: Eunice De Souza
places.passage: A teacher of English literature, who happens to be a single woman, musing on life and writing letters to an old flame Penguin

places.author: Eunice De Souza
places.passage: A married couple in Mumbai, and their circle of friends Penguin

places.author: N.P. Hafiz Muhammed
places.passage: DC Books

places.author: Abraham Verghese
places.passage: A sprawling, three-generation family saga where at least one member in each generation dies by drowning Grove Press

places.author: Tania James
places.passage: A spellbinding historical novel set in the eighteenth century: a hero’s quest, a love story, the story of a young artist coming of age, and an exuberant heist adventure that traces the bloody legacy of colonialism across two continents and fifty years. Knopf

places.author: Gandhi Riyang
places.passage: The story explores a theme of ecological metamorphosis through the lens of magical realism, where the boundary between ancestral myth and environmental collapse dissolves. It depicts a natural world forced into a grotesque transformation to survive a landscape poisoned by greed, blending the supernatural with the visceral reality of a dying earth. This narrative serves as a warning that when the sacred bond between humanity and nature is betrayed, life must mutate into something haunting and unrecognizable just to endure. Published by Lekha in collaboration with the Centre for Contemporary Folklore (CCF)

places.author: Banu Mushtaq
places.passage: The lives of women and girls in South India Translated into English by Deepa Bhashti. This won the International Booker Prize in 2025.

places.author: Guruguhan Iyer
places.passage: "Shunya Vedana … Shunya Vedana … Shunya Vedana …". The chanting never stops at 7, Srujan Nivas. It looks like an ordinary flat, but is it? Ramana and Meenakshi move into their new home right after their wedding. Three days later, his parents visit them, drift off under the ceiling fan, and die in their sleep. Two decades later, Meenakshi meets the same fate. Their son Venkat realises his family's living the last phase of their lives over and over. Shunya and Vedana, the self-proclaimed Gods, promise liberation in exchange for Venkat's complete surrender. Will he yield or is there something far bigger at play? The story is set in Matunga, Mumbai. The restaurants, shops and temples mentioned are real.

places.author: Mahesh Rao
places.passage: Queer, identity, relationships Queer contemporary fiction about sexual repression and discovering sexuality, embedded very strongly in the settings of Darjeeling, and after a time jump, in Mumbai. The places feature strongly in the storyline, and add to the context of the narrative

places.author: Altaf Tyrewala (editor)
places.passage: The stories in Mumbai Noir depict the many ways in which the city's ever-present shadowy aspects often force themselves onto the lives of ordinary people.When viewed from a plane (or hot-air balloon), any metropolis might strike one as jaw-dropping. For a majority of Mumbai's residents, however, the city's overcrowded public transportation and decaying infrastructure fail to provide even the minimum of relief.

places.author: Dr. Aparna Salvi Nagda
places.passage: The novel explores the lives of three individuals trapped in silence against the backdrop of changing sociopolitical scenario of Belgaum in the late 90s.

places.author: Daribha Lyndem
places.passage: In this novella, Daribha Lyndem gently lifts the curtain on the coming of age of a young Khasi woman and the politically charged city of Shillong in which she lives. Like the beloved school game from which she takes the name, the book meanders through ages, lives, and places. The interconnected stories build on each other to cover the breadth of childhood, and move into the precarious awareness of adulthood.

places.bookTitle: We come from the Mist: Writings from the Meghalaya
places.author: Edited by Janice Pariat
Sunil MS
places.passage: There is a whale in the city of Bangalore and only the Listeners can hear it. The Wanderer, a man suffering from dissociative amnesia, is sucked into this secret world of the Listeners and joins their search for the mysterious whale. He is unable to hear the song of the whale that beckons the Listeners every night. Filled with longing for the music, the Wanderer finds strange new friendships and love in this bizarre world of the Listeners. But the world he has built, sits on the shoulder of a lie. Caught between the impenetrable wall of his past and the promise of a future, he turns to the Song of the Whale to set him free.

places.author: Sunil MS
places.passage:
Reality cracks. Time leaks. The world will end in 102 days.
A message appears on every digital device on Earth: reality is a simulation―and it's shutting down. As the world descends into chaos and eerie “glitches” begin to warp the fabric of existence, five strangers―a jaded journalist, an atheist novelist, a love-struck cabbie, the Prime Minister of India, and a lonely bartender―grapple with the meaning of life, memory, and love in a universe that may not be real. But if nothing is real, what do we hold on to?

places.author: Ahmed Ali
places.passage: The novel is set around 1911 to 1919 in Delhi. Ahmed Ali has vividly drawn the picture of old Delhi and its Muslim inhabitants of that era. He depicts the themes of disintegration, degeneration, alienation, gender and social conflicts, nostalgia, the downfall of the Mughal emperors, and the effects of colonialism and imperialism on Indian Muslims in Delhi.
